Name: info_theoretic_quantization
Title: Information-Theoretic Limits of Neural Network Quantization for Low-Latency Inference
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the fundamental limit of neural network quantization for low-latency inference is determined by the information-theoretic tradeoff between the number of bits required to represent the model and the resulting distortion in the output. Specifically, we aim to derive a rate-distortion theory for neural networks that characterizes the minimum number of bits required to achieve a given level of accuracy.
Related Work: Recent works such as Spectral Quantization Routing and Geometric Signal Separation have explored the use of geometric and information-theoretic methods for neural network compression and quantization. However, these works have focused on empirical methods and heuristics, rather than deriving fundamental limits. Our work aims to fill this gap by establishing a rigorous information-theoretic framework for understanding the limits of neural network quantization.
Abstract: We propose a novel framework for understanding the information-theoretic limits of neural network quantization for low-latency inference. By casting quantization as a lossy source coding problem, we derive a rate-distortion theory that characterizes the minimum number of bits required to achieve a given level of accuracy. Our framework provides a rigorous and principled approach to quantization, allowing for the derivation of fundamental limits and the evaluation of practical quantization schemes. We demonstrate the applicability of our framework through experiments on benchmark datasets, showing that our approach can be used to optimize quantization schemes and improve inference efficiency.
Experiments: We will conduct experiments on benchmark datasets to evaluate the effectiveness of our information-theoretic framework for neural network quantization. Specifically, we will: (1) derive the rate-distortion curve for a given neural network architecture and dataset, (2) compare the performance of our framework with existing quantization schemes, and (3) evaluate the tradeoff between the number of bits required and the resulting distortion in the output. We will use metrics such as accuracy, bits-per-parameter, and inference latency to evaluate the performance of our framework.
Risk Factors and Limitations: One potential risk factor is that our framework may not be directly applicable to all types of neural networks or datasets. Additionally, our experiments may be limited by the availability of computational resources and the size of the datasets used. Furthermore, our framework assumes a fixed quantization scheme, and exploring the optimal quantization scheme for a given dataset and architecture is a topic for future work.
